1: Aaron is called out from among the Israelites for priestly office — not self-appointed but divinely summoned. Hebrews 5:4-5 uses the same logic to establish Christ's high priesthood: 'No one takes this honor on himself, but he receives it when called by God, just as Aaron was' — then immediately: 'In the same way, Christ did not take on himself the glory of becoming a high priest. But God said to him, You are my Son...' The Aaron-pattern of divine call provides the typological framework within which the Son's eternal appointment is understood.
2: 'Make sacred garments for your brother Aaron to give him dignity and honor.' The vestiture confers dignity (kavod) and honor (tifaret) on the one who wears it. Philippians 2:9-11: 'God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name.' The high priest's dignified vestments are the type of the glorified humanity of the risen Christ — the human nature that was stripped at the cross is now clothed in indestructible resurrection glory. The sacred garments anticipate the white garments of the victorious Christ (Revelation 1:13-16).
3: 'Tell all the skilled workers to whom I have given wisdom in such matters that they are to make garments for Aaron.' The craftsmen work with divinely given wisdom (chokhmah). The parallel in the new covenant is the Spirit-given gifts that build the church: 'To each one the manifestation of the Spirit is given for the common good' (1 Corinthians 12:7). The tabernacle is built by Spirit-empowered craftsmen; the church, the new temple, is built by Spirit-gifted workers each contributing their part to the single body (Ephesians 4:11-16).
4: The list of priestly garments: breastpiece, ephod, robe, woven tunic, turban, and sash. The comprehensive vestiture of the high priest covers the whole person — from head (turban) to body (tunic) to waist (sash) — signifying the total consecration of the priest for service. Christ's high-priestly qualification is similarly total: he is 'holy, blameless, pure, set apart from sinners, exalted above the heavens' (Hebrews 7:26). No partial consecration — every aspect of his humanity is set apart for the priestly work.
5: The materials — gold, blue, purple, scarlet yarn, and fine linen — are the same materials used for the tabernacle veil (Exodus 26:31) and the outer screen (Exodus 27:16). The color sequence represents a theological gradient: gold (divine glory), blue (heavenly), purple (royal), scarlet (sacrificial blood), fine linen (human purity). Christ unites all these registers: he is the divine glory enfleshed, the heavenly king who came to earth, the royal Son who shed his blood, the sinless human ('fine linen of righteous acts,' Revelation 19:8) who is our great High Priest.
6: The ephod is made of gold and colored yarns — a work of a 'skilled craftsman' (choshev, literally 'thinker-artisan'). The ephod served as an oracular garment (Judges 8:27; 1 Samuel 23:9-12) — the device through which divine guidance was sought. Christ as the final word of God (Hebrews 1:1-2) replaces the ephod's oracular function: 'In the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets... but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.' The ephod-oracle gives way to the Son-oracle who is himself the Word.
7: Two shoulder pieces of the ephod attached at its corners so it can be fastened. The shoulder in Hebrew imagery is the place of government and burden-bearing: 'The government will be on his shoulders' (Isaiah 9:6). The high priest's ephod, fastened at the shoulders, is the type of the royal-priestly authority that rests on the Messiah's shoulders. He bears the weight of governance (messianic king) and the weight of intercession (high priest) simultaneously — roles the Aaronic priesthood always held separately.
8: The waistband of the ephod — of one piece with it. The unity of the ephod and its waistband represents the integrated nature of the high-priestly office. In Christ, the priestly and kingly functions are organically united — not two offices awkwardly combined but one person who is, by nature, both Son-of-David-king and Melchizedekian-priest (Psalms 110:1-4; Hebrews 7:1-3). Zechariah 6:13 explicitly anticipates this: 'It is he who will build the temple... and he will be clothed with majesty and will sit and rule on his throne. And he will be a priest on his throne.'
9: Two onyx stones, engraved with the names of the twelve sons of Israel. The twelve names of the covenant people are borne on the high priest's shoulders into the divine presence. Jesus in John 17 prays for his disciples by name and by situation — the high-priestly prayer is the NT equivalent of Aaron carrying the names before God. 'He always lives to intercede for them' (Hebrews 7:25): the perpetual intercession of Christ before the Father is the antitype of Aaron bearing the twelve names into the holy place.
10: Six names on one stone, six on the other, in birth order. The precise, complete enumeration — no tribe left out, each in its proper place — is the type of Christ's individualized, non-generic intercession. He does not intercede for a vague category called 'the saved' but for specific persons: 'I pray for them. I am not praying for the world, but for those you have given me' (John 17:9). And later: 'I pray also for those who will believe in me through their message' (John 17:20). Every name is on the breastpiece.
11: The names engraved 'like a seal' (pituchei chotam) — permanent, official, indelible. The image of the seal on the high priest's stones anticipates Paul's language about the Spirit's sealing of believers: 'Having believed, you were marked in him with a seal, the promised Holy Spirit' (Ephesians 1:13). The believer is as permanently and officially marked as the name engraved on the high priest's stone — not written in sand but cut in stone, carried before God on the shoulders of the eternal priest.
12: 'Aaron is to bear the names before the LORD on his two shoulders as a memorial.' The high priest is the living memorial — his body carries the names of the people into the divine presence as he performs the sacrificial work. Christ in his resurrection body bears the marks of the cross — 'the marks of the Lord Jesus' (Galatians 6:17) — as the permanent memorial of the sacrifice he made for his people. His intercession at the Father's right hand is grounded in what his body accomplished: 'Because of the blood of his cross' (Colossians 1:20).
13: Gold filigree settings for the onyx stones. The stones are secured in the most valuable and workmanlike setting. The security of the high priest's memorial stones is the type of the security of those whom Christ bears before God: 'I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28). The gold filigree that holds the stones in place is the type of the unbreakable setting in which the Father holds those the Son intercedes for.
14: Two braided chains of pure gold, like ropes, attached to the filigree settings. The chains connect the breastpiece (intercession) to the shoulder settings (bearing). The connection between advocacy and presence is structural in the high-priestly vestments. Jesus's intercessory work is grounded in his atoning work — he intercedes 'on the basis of' the cross (Hebrews 9:12): it is because he 'entered the most holy place once for all by his own blood' that he now 'lives to intercede' (Hebrews 7:25).
15: 'Fashion a breastpiece for making decisions' (choshen mishpat — literally 'breastpiece of judgment/decision'). The breastpiece is the instrument of divine guidance and judicial decision — the oracle worn over the heart. Christ is 'the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8) — the one through whom God's final word of decision and judgment has come. Hebrews 4:12-13: 'the word of God... judges the thoughts and attitudes of the heart'; Christ as the Word incarnate is himself the living breastpiece of divine judgment over humanity.
16: The breastpiece is to be 'square — a span long and a span wide — and folded double.' The square shape suggests completeness and equal-sided justice; the doubling suggests fullness. In Revelation 21:16, the new Jerusalem is a perfect cube — equal in length, width, and height — the ultimate sacred space where the divine presence dwells fully. The square breastpiece is the embryonic form of the cubic holy-of-holies (1 Kings 6:20) which is the type of the perfect sacred space of the new creation.
17: The first row of stones on the breastpiece: carnelian (odem), chrysolite (pitdah), and beryl (bareketh). These same stones appear in the description of Eden's covering cherub in Ezekiel 28:13 and in the foundations of the new Jerusalem in Revelation 21:19-20. The high-priestly breastpiece connects the paradise of Eden (the beginning) through the Sinai covenant (the center) to the new Jerusalem (the end) — a single gemological thread running through the entire redemptive narrative.
18: The second row: turquoise (nofech), lapis lazuli (sapir), and moonstone (yahalom). The lapis lazuli appears in Ezekiel's vision of the divine throne (Ezekiel 1:26; Exodus 10:1) and in Exodus 24:10 (the pavement of sapphire under YHWH's feet when Moses and the elders ate with him). The breastpiece worn over Aaron's heart thus includes the stone of the divine throne — the high priest's very vestment contains a fragment of the vision of God's glory.
19: The third row: jacinth (leshem), agate (shevo), and amethyst (achlamah). The purple and violet tones of amethyst belong to the royal color range of the vestments (blue, purple, scarlet). The priestly breastpiece is simultaneously royal — the priest before God is also a king, anticipating the one who is 'King of kings and Lord of lords' (Revelation 19:16) and who makes his people 'a kingdom and priests to serve his God and Father' (Revelation 1:6).
20: The fourth row: chrysolite (tarshish), onyx (shoham), and jasper (yashfeh). The jasper stone appears as the first foundation of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:19) and as the appearance of the one on the throne (Revelation 4:3: 'had the appearance of jasper and ruby'). The breastpiece thus ends with a stone that, in Revelation's vision, represents the very appearance of God — the high priest's vestment, worn over his heart, points to the divine glory that it covers and mediates.
21: 'There are to be twelve stones, one for each of the names of the sons of Israel, each engraved like a seal with the name of one of the twelve tribes.' The names on the breastpiece are the names over the high priest's heart — not just on his shoulders (Exodus 28:12) but over his heart. The distinction between shoulder-bearing and heart-bearing is significant: the shoulders speak of intercessory work (bearing the weight); the heart speaks of intercessory love. Christ bears his people both with the strength of his priestly work and with the love of his heart (John 13:1: 'having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end').
22: Braided chains of pure gold for the breastpiece. The chains that bind the breastpiece to the ephod and to the shoulders are the structural elements that keep the heart-memorial in its proper place before God. Paul in Romans 8:38-39 expresses the chains that connect the believer to God: 'neither death nor life, neither angels nor demons, neither the present nor the future... will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord.' The chains of gold are the type of the unbreakable bonds of divine love.
23: Gold rings at the corners of the breastpiece attach the chains. The structural redundancy — multiple rings, multiple chains, connections at multiple points — ensures the breastpiece cannot slip or fall out of place. The intercession of Christ has the same structural redundancy: the Father's election, the Son's atonement, the Spirit's sealing, and Christ's ongoing intercession all work together to ensure that none who are given to Christ will be lost (John 6:39; Exodus 10:28-29; Romans 8:28-30).
24: The gold chains attach to the rings at the corners of the breastpiece. Each connection point is secure. The chain-and-ring imagery of attachment is reflected in Peter's warning about false teachers who 'have escaped the corruption of the world' but are then tangled again — contrasted with the true chains that bind believers to Christ through genuine transformation (2 Peter 1:4). The gold chains of the breastpiece represent the real, structural attachment of the people to their high priest — not superficial but built into the very vestments.
25: The other ends of the chains attach to the filigree settings on the shoulder pieces of the ephod. The breastpiece hangs between the shoulder-work and the heart — between the bearing of names (shoulder) and the carrying of names over the heart. This double attachment means that the memorial before God is both a matter of strength (shoulders = priestly work) and of love (heart = priestly affection). Christ's intercession is not merely official duty but arises from love: 'I pray for them' (John 17:9) — not a legal obligation but a heart-engagement.
26: Two more gold rings at the other two corners of the breastpiece, on the inside edge. The double-ring structure at each corner distributes the load, preventing any single point of failure. The priestly work of Christ is similarly structurally redundant: 'by two unchangeable things, in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us may be greatly encouraged' (Hebrews 6:18). The divine oath and the divine promise are the two rings that secure the breastpiece of hope.
27: Rings on the bottom of the shoulder pieces, connected to the breastpiece's waistband. The connecting structure becomes increasingly complex — each connection made doubly secure. The Epistle to the Hebrews similarly elaborates the multiple guarantees of Christ's high priesthood: the oath of Psalms 110:4, the resurrection, the indestructible life, the permanent priesthood. The accumulation of guarantees is not redundancy for its own sake but the reflection of the gold-chains-and-rings structure of the high-priestly vestments.
28: The rings of the breastpiece are tied to the rings of the ephod with blue cord, connecting the breastpiece to the waistband so it will not swing away. The breastpiece must remain directly over the heart — not free to swing out of place. The concern is that the names of the people should always remain over the high priest's heart. Christ's intercession is permanent and continuous — 'he always lives to intercede for them' (Hebrews 7:25). The blue cord that keeps the breastpiece from swinging away is the type of the unending, constant nature of the Son's mediation.
29: 'Whenever Aaron enters the holy place, he will bear the names of the sons of Israel over his heart on the breastpiece of decision as a continual memorial before the LORD.' The culminating verse of the breastpiece instruction: the names are over the heart, the approach to God is regular ('whenever'), the bearing is personal (Aaron himself), and the purpose is memorial intercession. Hebrews 7:25: 'He always lives to intercede for them' — the 'always' of Christ's intercession is the antitype of the 'whenever Aaron enters' — not periodic but perpetual, because the risen high priest never leaves the divine presence.
30: 'Also put the Urim and the Thummim in the breastpiece, so they may be over Aaron's heart whenever he enters the presence of the LORD. Thus Aaron will always bear the means of making decisions for the Israelites over his heart before the LORD.' The Urim and Thummim — 'lights and perfections' or 'yes and no' (from LXX) — are the binary oracular device by which YHWH communicated his will in the period before written prophecy. Christ as the final Word of God (Hebrews 1:1-2) replaces the Urim and Thummim: 'In the past God spoke... through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son.' The oracle has become incarnate; the divine will is no longer accessed through a breastpiece but through a Person.
31: 'Make the robe of the ephod entirely of blue cloth.' The robe — all blue (t'khelet) — is the color of heaven and the covenant (Numbers 15:38-40: the blue thread on tassels to remind Israel of the commandments). Christ in Revelation 1:13 is described 'dressed in a robe reaching down to his feet' — the priestly robe of the eternal high priest. The all-blue robe beneath the colorful ephod layers the heavenly identity under the sacrificial work: the one who comes to earth in the colorful vestments of incarnate priesthood is first and last the heavenly Son.
32: The woven collar around the robe's neck opening, like a soldier's armor coat, so it will not tear. The robe 'must not tear' — the integrity of the high-priestly vestment is protected. John 19:24 notes that the soldiers cast lots for Jesus's tunic 'which was seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom,' and decided not to tear it. The seamless tunic of Jesus is widely read as the type of the high-priestly robe that must not be torn — the high priest's vestment that is preserved whole even in the hour of crucifixion.
33: Pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn around the hem of the robe, with gold bells between them. The pomegranate, with its many seeds enclosed in one skin, is a traditional symbol of the covenant community — one people with many members united under one covering. The bells sound as the priest moves. Together, the pomegranates (the people) and the bells (the sound of presence) speak of the priestly vocation: gathering the people (pomegranates) and announcing the approach of God (bells).
34: The alternating gold bells and pomegranates around the hem. The pattern of gold bells and pomegranates creates a rhythmic alternation of divine sound and human/community symbol. The worship of the new-covenant community follows the same alternating pattern: divine word (the bell, the proclamation) and human response (the pomegranate, the gathered community). Liturgy is this back-and-forth — God speaks (bell), the people respond (pomegranate) — the pattern stitched into the hem of the high-priestly robe.
35: 'Aaron must wear it when he ministers. The sound of the bells will be heard when he enters the holy place before the LORD and when he comes out, so that he will not die.' The bells signal the high priest's approach so that his entry is known and prepared — so that the holiness of God does not consume him unannounced. Hebrews 10:19-22 describes the new-covenant boldness that replaces this mortal anxiety: 'We have confidence to enter the most holy place by the blood of Jesus... let us draw near.' The bells' function — warning of the potentially lethal approach — is abolished in Christ, who opens permanent, confident access to the divine presence.
36: 'Make a plate of pure gold and engrave on it as you would engrave a seal: HOLY TO THE LORD.' The tzitz (golden plate) engraved with qodesh laYHWH — 'Holy to YHWH' — is the culminating element of the high-priestly vestiture. The high priest's very forehead declares the identity of his master and the purpose of his service. Zechariah 14:20-21 uses this same inscription eschatologically: 'On that day HOLY TO THE LORD will be inscribed on the bells of the horses... every cooking pot in Jerusalem and Judah will be holy to the LORD.' The inscription on the high priest's forehead is the seed of the universal holiness that pervades the new creation.
37: The golden plate is attached by a blue cord to the front of the turban. The turban (crown-equivalent for the high priest, since priests do not wear military crowns) bears the declaration of holiness at the most visible and noble point — the forehead. Jesus is the 'Holy One of God' (John 6:69; Mark 1:24) — his holiness is not a garment he wears but his very nature. The tzitz on Aaron's forehead declares what Aaron mediates; the person of Christ is what the tzitz points to.
38: 'It will be on Aaron's forehead, and he will bear the guilt involved in the sacred gifts the Israelites consecrate, whatever their sacred gifts may be. It will be on Aaron's forehead continually so that they will be acceptable to the LORD.' The high priest bears the avon ha-kodashim — the guilt of the holy things. Even Israel's best worship is tainted — the sacred gifts carry residual guilt that must be absorbed by the high priest before they are accepted. This is the deepest type of the cross: Christ bears not only Israel's sins but the imperfection of their best offerings, so that even their imperfect worship is accepted because of his perfect mediation.
39: 'Weave the tunic of fine linen and make the turban of fine linen. The sash is to be the work of an embroiderer.' Fine linen (shesh) is the material of the holy — used for the tabernacle curtains, the priestly garments, and in Revelation 19:8 for the bridal garment of the church: 'Fine linen stands for the righteous acts of God's holy people.' The fine-linen tunic of the high priest is the type of the righteousness in which Christ clothes his people — not their own righteousness but his (Philippians 3:9; Zechariah 3:4-5).
40: 'Make tunics, sashes and caps for Aaron's sons to give them dignity and honor.' All the priests are dignified — not just the high priest. The NT extends high-priestly dignity to the whole community: 'You are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation' (1 Peter 2:9). Every believer in Christ is clothed in priestly dignity. The tunics of Aaron's sons, conferring honor on those who serve in the outer courts, are the type of the white garments given to all who overcome (Revelation 3:5; Exodus 7:9).
41: 'After you put these clothes on your brother Aaron and his sons, anoint and ordain them (male' yadam — fill their hands). Consecrate them so they may serve me as priests.' The ordination formula — male' yad (fill the hand) — is the investiture with office. Acts 10:38: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power.' The Spirit's anointing of Jesus at his baptism is the NT ordination — his priestly and royal office is conferred by the Father's anointing, not by a human ceremony. The filling of the hand (with the sacrificial portions to wave before God) is the type of the filling of the Spirit that equips for priestly service.
42: 'Make linen undergarments as a covering for the body, reaching from the waist to the thigh.' The undergarments address the most basic concern: the covering of the body's vulnerability before God. The nakedness that Adam and Eve covered with fig leaves (Genesis 3:7) was addressed at the sanctuary gate: YHWH made them garments of skin (Genesis 3:21). The priestly linen undergarments continue this covering — the exposure of the human before the holy God requires divine provision of covering. Christ's righteousness is the ultimate covering: 'clothe yourselves with the Lord Jesus Christ' (Romans 13:14).
43: 'Aaron and his sons must wear them whenever they enter the tent of meeting or approach the altar to minister in the holy place, so that they will not incur guilt and die.' The vestments are not decorative but protective — their absence means death. The clothing of Christ's righteousness is similarly protective, not merely aesthetic: 'without holiness no one will see the Lord' (Hebrews 12:14). The linen undergarments that prevent priestly death are the type of the imputed righteousness that allows sinners to approach the holy God without being consumed.
